titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Asa Fitch Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Janet Elaine Gertz

conventiondeclaration.abbr: finding aid created in accordance with manuscripts and archives' processing manual.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday August 30, 2018 at 21:26
finding aid revisiion description not supplied

did.unittitle: Asa Fitch papers
did.abstract: Twenty-one diaries, 1821-1879, of Asa Fitch, scientist and entomologist. The diaries reflect in considerable detail Fitch's life and thoughts from early adolescence through his education at the Rensselaer School in Troy, New York and the Vermont Academy of Medicine in Castleton, his career as a scientist and state entomologist in New York, and his retirement. Also included are papers and genealogical materials relating to other members of the Fitch family.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Asa Fitch Papers (MS 215). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of the estate of Julian M. Sturtevant, 1952.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: The entire collection is available on microfilm (2,802 frames on 4 reels, 35mm.) from Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library, at cost. Order no. HM141.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Asa Fitch Papers consist of fifty items. Twenty-one of these are diaries kept by Asa Fitch from 1821 through 1879 in various places in New York State and elsewhere. Notations for several years are lacking. Six volumes, 1821-1828, were lettered A through G, with B missing. To the remaining fifteen volumes numbers have arbitrarily been assigned, although entries are not always consecutive by date.
The remainder of the collection is made up of Fitch family genealogical material copied from the notes of the father of Abbie M. (Fitch) Andrews (1840-) and notes taken from her own research.
A detailed description of the contents of the diaries and other papers follows.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Practiced medicine, 1831-1838; studied economic entomology and agriculture, and began publishing articles on the subject in 1845; in 1854 appointed state entomologist of New York, remaining until 1871.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.
The entire collection is available on microfilm. Patrons must use FILM HM 141 instead of the originals.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection are in the public domain. There are no restrictions on use. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

did.unittitle: Inventory
Diaries of Asa Fitch
Volume "A": Kept in Bennington, VT, places in Connecticut, including New Haven, in Salem, Cambridge, Jackson, Saratoga Springs, Albany, New York City, and Troy.
Volume "B" [no volume]

Volume "C": An account of a scientific tour to Lake Erie under the direction of Prof. Amos Eaton. The entire company is listed in entry for May 2, 1826. For another account of this trip, see written description (April 26, 1826), box 3.
Volume "D": Diary of his first term at Rensselaer School (later Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute) in Troy; also 6 p. of expense accounts.
Volume "E": School life in Troy and sojourns in Salem, Greenwich, Albany, Watervliet, Whitecreek, and places in VT, including Bennington; also 2 p. of expense accounts.
Volume "F": Diary of his first year at Castleton Medical School, Castleton, N.Y.; and sojourn in Salem, Greenwich, Granville, and Troy; also 6 p. of expense accounts.
Volume "G": Kept in Whitecreek, Hoosick, Lanesboro, Williamstown, Salem, Greenwich and New York City; also 6 p. of expense accounts.
Volume 1: Kept in New York City, Harlem, Manhattanville, Fort Washington, Tarrytown, Singsing, Peekskill, Poughkeepsie, Albany, Whitecreek, Salem, Greenwich, etc.; also 5 p. of expense accounts.
Volume 2: Kept in Salem, Greenwich, Albany, Troy, Castleton, VT, etc.; also 6 p. of expense accounts.
Volume 3: Kept in Castleton, Vt., Salem, Granville, Hebron, Cambridge, Albany, Troy, Jackson.

Volume 4: Kept in Bennington, VT, and Salem, but mostly a scientific expedition with Rensselaer students, a list of whom is given, under Prof. Amos Eaton up the Hudson from New York to Troy, on the Mohawk to Rome, thence to New Boston and Green Lake; meeting with Daniel Cady Eaton in New York, 1830 Jun 23.
Volume 5: Kept in Jordan.
Volume 6: Kept in Greenville and Vandalia, IL.
Volume 7: Kept in Salem, Jackson, Point Penninsula, Union, Whitecreek, Cambridge, places in VT (particularly Brandon) and Argyle and Ft. Miller, NY.

Volume 8: First part explains lapse in time since previous entries. Latter part written over penciled lecture notes, 1829, few of which are legible. This volume is mainly a letter book, interspersed among diary entries, consisting of letters to members of his family, a few to others on matters of business, but most to Henry Bleecher, George F. Horton, or Wm. S. Robertson. Diary entries recorded in Salem, Albany (where he attended an abolition convention 1839 Aug 1), Stillwater, Saratoga Springs, Warrensburg.
Volume 9: Kept in Albany, New York City, Granville, Salem and places in NY on scientific expeditions and at various meetings.

Volume 10: Kept mainly in Salem with accounts of weather observations and scientific experiments; in Albany, where on Apr 25, 1865 he watched the funeral procession of Pres. Abraham Lincoln's remains; in New York City, where on Oct. 29, 1865 he heard Henry Ward Beecher speak in Plymouth Church Flatbush; and in Troy.
Volume 11: Kept in Albany, Wallingford, CT, Salem, and places in NY and VT where he attended agricultural meetings.
Volume 12: Kept in Salem, Manchester, VT, Albany and Troy.

Volume 13. Kept in Troy, where on Jun 28, 1871, he heard Prof. Hall speak at the Rensselaer Institute Alumni Meeting and where on Jun 16, 1871 he attended the 48th Annual Commencement of the Rensselaer Institute, a program of which is enclosed; in Salem and Albany.
Volume 14: Kept in Salem, Saratoga Springs, Albany, Utica, Verona, and New York City.
Volume 15: Kept in Castleton and Argyle, but mainly Salem; contains an alphabetical list of persons mentioned in the diaries.
Asa Fitch: other volumes
Autograph album, containing biographical material for many of the signers

Written description, Apr 26, 1826, of commencement at Rensselaer School [later Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute], followed by an account of a scientific tour to Lake Erie, May 2 - Jun 10, by the school under the direction of Prof. Amos Eaton. (Four folders of letter-paper numbered 2-5, lacking number 1, plus one fragment.) With these are two more unnumbered folders giving an account of commencement in 1828 and a geological tour up the Erie Canal to Rochester where "the most marked courtesies" were shown them by Thurlow Weed. Laid in is an amusing account of Prof. Rafinesque as a member of one of these expeditions.
Commonplace book of conundrums, maxims, proverbs, etc.
"Memorandum book" of his farm activities at Salem, lv.
Family members
Andrews, Abbie M. (Fitch). Genealogical data on the Fitch family
Fitch, Elizabeth (McNeil), (1812-). Autograph Album, 1 v.
Fitch, Martin, (1793-1816). Latin composition book [Began Jun 11, 1807] at Lansingburgh, N.Y., 1 v.

Rider, Benjamin W, (1844-1871). "A journal for wayside jottings in this journey of life" [i.e. his reminiscences, 17 p. numbered 5-21. Remainder of volume by his wife, Anna Olivia (Fitch) Rider Skinner
Skinner, Anna Olivia (Fitch) Rider, (1844-1925)
Genealogical notes on her family and that of her second husband, Lemuel Sherman Skinner, and their children. Written in volume of Benjamin W. Rider's reminiscences, 1 v.
"My Anthology" ; A combination scrapbook and commonplace book compiled by her. 1 v.
Paper entitled "Our Blessings" [6 p.].
Photograph-autograph album. 1 v.
Genealogy and miscellaneous material consisting mainly of newspaper clippings, postcard views, etc., removed from the various volumes
Reprints of articles by Samuel Rezneck relating to Asa Fitch's journals
